text: I usually have toast with scrambled eggs and a strong cuppa tea. Sometimes, if my son is coming round to visit, we might make some pancakes together with fresh fruit and syrup. On really busy weekdays, I'll grab something quick like yogurt and granola, maybe a slice of wholemeal bread with jam. Nothing too fancy, just simple comfort food to give me a bit of energy before starting my day.

My typical lunch is usually something simple, really. I like a warm meal, so most weeks I'll make myself a sandwich or leftovers from last night's dinner. Sometimes, if my son brings me some fruit or a bagel, he might join me for a bit. We have a nice little table set out near the window where we can chat while eating. If I'm feeling more energetic after our morning walk, I might pop into the local café nearby for a cuppa and maybe some toast or soup. It's not fancy, but it gives us a chance to catch up before I start tidying up afterwards.

I'm a bit of an early bird, I sleep quite early, actually. My bedtime is 10 pm, which has been my usual time for many years now. It helps me feel rested and ready for the morning, even though some people might think it's a bit late!

I think self-care is rather like tending to a garden, isn't it? You need to nurture yourself regularly so everything stays healthy and vibrant. For me, that starts with getting plenty of sleep – aiming for an early night, usually between 10 pm and bed. A gentle alarm clock wakes me up at 6:30 am, which gives me some extra time to collect my thoughts before starting the day. In the morning, I try to have something calming to begin with, perhaps a cuppa tea and a bit of reading. If the weather permits, taking a brisk walk outside helps too – fresh air does wonders for clearing one's mind! After breakfast, I fit in some exercise, whether it's stretching or a spot of yoga; nothing strenuous, though! Throughout the day, I make sure to take breaks whenever I feel overwhelmed or struggling with concentration. Sometimes it's just a quick stretch or two while chatting with someone, other times more substantial breaks where I step away from whatever task requires focus and recharge elsewhere. And during those periods of quiet contemplation, I find writing down what comes into my head helps clarify any worries or concerns. Before dinner, I tidy up the house a bit, making sure every room looks its best, even if only for

I make an effort to be approachable and friendly, which seems to put people at ease. When attending meetings or interacting with colleagues, I listen actively and ask open-ended questions to encourage discussion. If someone comes to me with a query or issue, I try to offer helpful guidance while keeping them informed about any progress or changes. Sometimes, we'll grab a cuppa together during breaks to chat informally – those little moments always seem to foster stronger relationships. Occasionally, our team organises informal gatherings like birthday celebrations or Christmas parties, where everyone gets a chance to mingle. It's lovely to catch up with others outside of work-related contexts. In my experience, these sort of occasions have helped build trust and camaraderie among us. As part of the training sessions, we had guest speakers come in who shared valuable insights into different aspects of their fields. These presentations really resonated with many of us, so afterwards, there'd usually be some lively debates and exchanges amongst ourselves. We even formed a little support network for one another to share tips, advice, or simply lend an ear when needed. Networking isn't something I'm extremely skilled at, but being genuine, empathetic, and genuinely interested in what others have to say has led to some rewarding connections and collaborations

Well, since my retirement, I have more flexibility with my daily routine, which helps me manage my productivity. You know, getting up a bit earlier gives me a nice morning routine going – making some tea, having a quick tidy of the house... gets everything sorted before starting work. I find writing things down first thing sets me up nicely. Lists what needs tackling, prioritises tasks, breaks them into smaller chunks if needed. Then I usually spend most of the morning responding to emails, tidying up paperwork or attending virtual meetings via Zoom. It really depends on how much of each task is required, so sometimes we might only need half an hour to catch-up on something. My grandkids like keeping me company during these sessions too! In between those focused blocks of time, I try taking a break to take a walk outside – fresh air does wonders! Or perhaps grab another cuppa and read something – always looking forward to catching up on history books or watching a documentary afterwards. And of course, there's the afternoon dip where everyone says everyone should be feeling refreshed, recharged and ready to tackle more tasks again. But honestly, it's all about finding your rhythm – mine tends to ebb-and-flow throughout the day rather than being one big block of steady focus

I find video calls quite helpful, especially now that my grandson is getting older and we can catch up properly. However, I must admit I'm still a bit wary of them. Sometimes the technology gets on my nerves – all those beeps and notifications! But, I know how much our grandkids rely on me being available for virtual chats, so I make an effort to join in whenever I can. When using my iPad, which has been kindly lent to me by one of my grandsons, I have to ask him to walk me through any tricky bits first. He's very patient with me, bless him!

I think what helps me is having a regular daily routine, even though my workdays aren't structured like they used to be. I make sure to have something planned out for myself each morning, whether it's going for a walk, tending to my garden, or simply enjoying a cuppa while catching up on some reading. Having these little rituals gives me a sense of purpose and gets me moving throughout the day. Plus, knowing exactly how much free time I'll have available allows me to plan leisurely outings with friends or spend more time with my grandkids – all of which brings its own joy!

I think one has to be quite flexible, really. In my previous jobs, we'd have set objectives, and sometimes circumstances would dictate a shift in focus. It was essential to remain adaptable and adjust our strategies accordingly. You wouldn't always know what the future held, so being able to pivot wasn't an option – you had to anticipate changes and react promptly. It's rather like tending to your garden; some seasons bring more growth than others, and you need to respond accordingly. If something isn't thriving, you prune and nurture it differently until conditions improve. Similarly, in work, prioritising might mean shifting resources or reassigning responsibilities temporarily while you attend to pressing issues. The key is staying focused on the bigger picture and making informed decisions based on available information.

I'd say it's all about being clear, concise, and assertive while still being polite and professional, of course! When I need something done differently or have a concern, I'll usually speak directly with my line manager or supervisor, explaining why I think changes should be made and what benefits there might be to them. I'm quite particular about getting things done properly, so I like to make sure everyone understands how I expect things to be handled. If I feel someone isn't taking me seriously because of my age, I'll try to nip that in the bud straight away – remind them who I am, where I come from, and what I know. Age is nothing, and I intend to prove myself every single day! At one point, we had an issue with staffing shortages here at the GP surgery, and some people were saying women weren't suited for certain positions due to stereotypes. But I said, "Not true!" It was simply a matter of finding capable individuals regardless of sex. We worked hard together, and eventually, we sorted out those issues. My colleagues came round once they saw our team's capabilities. It takes confidence and conviction to stand up for oneself, but trust me, it pays off in the end. You never know unless you give it

When I feel like I'm being misunderstood, I try to take a deep breath and remind myself that we all have our unique perspectives, and what might seem obvious to one person may not be so to another. If someone asks me again and again why I think something is the way it is, I'll explain my reasoning slowly and clearly, hoping that they'll finally grasp where I'm coming from. Sometimes, I need more time to gather my thoughts before responding; other times, a bit of humor can diffuse any tension. But most importantly, I ask them to tell me how they're feeling – maybe there's been some miscommunication! We usually end up having a lovely chat afterwards, and everything clears up. After all, isn't that what relationships and conversations are all about?

I love spending time with my loved ones! During family gatherings, we usually sit around the table together, sharing a meal and catching up on each other's lives. We tend to chat about everything from work and hobbies to current events and our grandkids' latest antics. My granddaughter is always eager to share her drawings and stories, so those moments are precious indeed! If there aren't any meals involved, I might take them out for a walk or play some board games together – Scrabble or Rummikub are favourites among us. Occasionally, we'll have a game night where everyone brings along a favourite dish to share. Those evenings are great opportunities to bond over laughter and shared memories. It's lovely being surrounded by people who know me best; conversations flow effortlessly, and everyone feels welcome. Family gatherings bring such joy and comfort, reminding me of what truly matters in life.

Honestly, I'm not too keen on these modern platforms like LinkedIn. In my opinion, they're a bit too focused on networking and self-promotion. As someone who values authenticity and keeping a lower profile, I find all that fuss around building an online presence quite overwhelming. Give me a good cuppa, some decent company, and a chat about real-life topics any day! My son says I should be more present on them, what with everyone using them nowadays, but I think I'll stick to our local village newsletter and the occasional parish magazine – much more suitable for me, thank you very much!

I think I'm a fairly straightforward sort of person, so I tend to speak my mind when discussing difficult subjects, while still being considerate of other people's feelings. If someone asks me directly how I feel or what I think about something, I'll usually tell them honestly, even if it might be uncomfortable for either party involved. However, before bringing up certain issues, I like to make sure we're all sitting comfortably and have had enough tea first! It helps set a gentle tone for open conversation. Once I know everyone is prepared, I try to listen actively and respond thoughtfully rather than jumping into an argument. Of course, there may be occasions where tact takes precedence – I've found that sometimes it's better to wait until later to discuss more delicate matters. But generally speaking, I believe having these sorts of conversations openly allows us to work through our differences together more effectively. After all, isn't that what friendships and relationships are really all about, eh?

I'd say I'm quite stubborn when it comes to standing my ground once I've made a decision, which might be misinterpreted as being resistant to peer pressure. But honestly, I think it's more about knowing what's best for myself and sticking to those principles rather than letting others sway me one way or another. If someone's opinion is different, we'll have a friendly discussion, and maybe even agree to disagree – but ultimately, I trust my instincts.

I try to steer clear of them, to be honest. They seem so intrusive and annoying, and I find myself clicking away out of frustration more often than not. My son always laughs at me saying how much I dislike adverts, but he knows I'm a bit like that. If something isn't relevant to what I need or interest, there's little point having it bombard me all the time. And I suppose my lack of technical know-how makes it hard enough; adding constant distractions feels overwhelming! Give me a simple list of information any day – preferably one I already knew anyway!

I must admit, managing multiple browser tabs isn't my strong point, to be honest. To me, a clean desktop is essential. If I have too many open, it becomes overwhelming. So, I stick to having maybe two or three windows open at any given time – usually one for emails, another for news or a website I need information from, and perhaps a third for something more leisurely like browsing recipe websites while cooking. It helps me stay focused and avoid getting lost in endless scrolling. My granddaughter always says I'm quite tidy with my computer setup!

Well, I think anyone who feels the need to be a troll is usually coming from a place of insecurity themselves, aren't they? It's like they're trying to bring others down so they feel better about themselves. As for me, I try to rise above it all. If someone leaves a nasty comment, I'll either ignore them or politely point out how unhelpful it was and move on. You know, it's amazing how much more pleasant your day becomes once you focus on constructive conversation rather than letting negativity weigh you down. My grandmother always said "a bit of dirt never hurts, but too much will rot everything." So, I figure, why let the little jabs bother me?

I'm a bit embarrassed to admit it, but I usually end up calling my grandchild, Jack, who's very handy with computers. He always manages to troubleshoot whatever is wrong and gets me sorted out again. It's lovely having him nearby, we have such nice chats while he fixes everything!

I like to think it's quite simple really. Every month, I sit down with a cuppa and go through my calendar to make sure I know what's due and how much each bill is going to be. Then, I sort them out into categories – utilities, council tax, pensions, etc., and work out which ones need to be paid first. Once I have all that sorted, I break them down into manageable chunks and set reminders on my... er... old-fashioned paper planner to help me remember. It helps having those little notes stuck somewhere visible so I'm reminded when payment's due. Also, I try to pay some bills ahead of time, like the utility bills, while others, like the council tax, might only come round once every quarter. And, of course, there's always something coming up that requires money, like buying groceries or petrol, but overall, I find keeping track works pretty smoothly for me. My grandkids even helped me set up automatic payments for certain regular expenses, like their birthdays and holidays, isn't that clever!

I take a bit of time to think about each one, usually sit down with my calendar and notes and make a list of all the subscription services we're paying for. Then I go through them one by one, asking myself why I signed up in the first place and whether I actually use it regularly enough. If it's something like streaming services, is there another service that does what I need more efficiently? If I haven't used it in six months, I consider cancelling it. But sometimes I might find out later that I forgot how much I was using it – so I'll hold off for three months instead. It's always nice to have some flexibility in case my tastes or circumstances change. And if someone else wants to share the cost, even better! So far, I'd say about half of our subscriptions end up getting cancelled because of lack of usage, while others remain simply because it's convenient or worth keeping for sentimental reasons.

I'd always say that I'm a saver at heart, so before making any big purchase like replacing my washing machine, I try to think very carefully about whether it's really necessary and what our budget will allow. We're fortunate enough now that we have some savings put away, which gives us peace of mind, but I still prefer to weigh all the pros and cons before splashing out. It might be helpful to make a little list and consider how much wear and tear the item gets, too – does someone else already know when it needs replacing? And what prices are similar items going for elsewhere? A bit of research usually helps me feel more sure-footed. Plus, there's something satisfying about knowing we've saved our money wisely!

I think it all comes down to what you're looking for really. If something is essential, like a new appliance or replacement parts, I'd definitely opt for brand new. But if it's something that's not crucial, such as furniture or books, I'm happy to consider second-hand options. You have to weigh the pros and cons – does it need to be perfect now, or will someone else find joy out of it afterwards? Plus, being a bit frugal was instilled in me during my youth, so I still try to make sense of every penny. Still, sometimes it feels more sensible to spend money upfront rather than worrying about saving up for an older model or scouring markets for bargains. It depends on the situation, you know?

Well, I think it all comes down to being mindful of one's spending habits and sticking to what works for me. Growing up during wartime rationing taught us a lot about making do with what we had, and our mum always said "waste not, want not." It stuck with me, really. When I was younger, my father used to say something similar – he'd say, "you're only getting out of your depth financially if you start borrowing money." So, whenever I need to make a purchase, I try to find ways to cut costs elsewhere first. We shop sales, buy second-hand items, and plan ahead for big-ticket purchases like replacing appliances or furniture. And once I have an idea of how much I'll spend, I stick to that amount tightly. My granddaughter says I'm very strict about budgets, but someone has to be! It's funny, sometimes people assume because I'm retired now, I must live comfortably off my state pension and savings. But actually, there's still so many expenses – bills, maintenance, and upkeep on the house... keeping track of everything is crucial. Still, I manage okay; I feel fortunate to be able to save some each month, even if it's not a fortune. Being frugal never

I love a bargain! Whenever there's a sale, my husband always says I'm like a kid on Christmas morning. I try to make a list beforehand so I know what we need to pick up during our regular shopping trips out. We tend to stick to essentials first – toiletries, cleaning supplies, maybe some winter clothing if needed. Then, I'll scan the shelves for any nice deals on books or kitchen gadgets I might have been eyeing. And, of course, I take advantage of discounts on groceries whenever I can. It's all about being sensible and staying on top of our finances, but still getting something extra for ourselves every now and then is lovely.

Portion control is quite simple for me, really. As a child growing up during wartime rationing, we were taught the value of making every bit count. My mum was an excellent cook who always made sure our meals stretched far enough to last us till the next shop visit. Even now, in my retirement, I still believe in eating mindfully and being aware of what I'm putting into my body. I find it helpful to measure out ingredients before cooking and make myself aware of serving sizes. A little goes a long way, and I try not to overindulge even in favourite treats like cakes and biscuits. If someone invites me for dinner, I'll politely decline seconds unless absolutely necessary – there's nothing worse than feeling guilty afterwards! When dining out, I opt for smaller portions whenever possible, too. My love of vegetables helps, though – there's so much goodness packed into them! They're filling and delicious, which makes it easier to feel satisfied without going overboard. Of course, birthdays and special occasions call for indulgence, but generally speaking, moderation has been my motto since those rationing days. It's served me well all these years, I think.

I think a more reflective, gentle approach is best for me. I'd prefer someone who encourages discussion and exploration rather than being too prescriptive or telling. You know, lots of people say what we should be doing instead of focusing on how we're feeling, so I find it helpful to talk about my thoughts and emotions first before offering advice. It's like the saying goes: "patience and curiosity" – sometimes taking an honest look at ourselves helps us figure out our own solutions better.

I think prevention is key, dear. As a morning lark, I find my productivity peaks between 8 am and noon, so I try to fit most of my work into those hours. Taking regular breaks during the day helps me recharge too – perhaps a cuppa, some fresh air, or a quick walk. And, of course, setting realistic expectations and prioritising tasks ensures I'm managing myself effectively rather than getting overwhelmed. Plus, having something to look forward to afterwards, like a lovely afternoon stroll or a nice cupcake, always gives me a bit of motivation to push through the difficult bits. It's all about balance, isn't it? A gentle pace that allows me to be productive while still taking care of myself.

I think a regular bedtime is key, so I try to be in bed by 10 pm sharp. I find it helps me wind down before I go to sleep. And I make sure my bedroom is dark, quiet, and cool – nothing like a cold room to encourage a restful night's sleep! It might sound simple, but sticking to a morning routine has helped too. I'm an early riser anyway, which suits me, and getting some fresh air first thing gets those endorphins going. Plus, having something to look forward to, whether it's tea and toast or just enjoying nature, gives me motivation to start the day feeling refreshed. My son says he wishes more people understood how much of a difference these little habits can make!

I try to mix it up quite a bit, really. You know, I used to be very much into swimming when I was younger, so now my morning walks are lovely, getting some fresh air and stretching out my legs is wonderful for me. In the winter months, I might take my dog along and we'll walk round the park together. Summer evenings are perfect for a stroll by the river; the sun sets beautifully over there. It's peaceful, you know? Occasionally, if I'm feeling more energetic, I might join one of those gentle yoga classes offered at our local community centre – nothing too strenuous, mind! The instructor has arthritis like myself, which makes her extra considerate of participants. Sometimes, if the weather permits, I'll go for bike rides on quieter roads nearby. Nothing too taxing, though! On Sundays, I usually tidy the garden while watching the telly – always something pleasant to listen to, even if it's an old episode of EastEnders! My grandkids think it's hilarious how Mrs Beeton still gets stuck in the kitchen sink! But yes, mixing things up helps prevent boredom and maintains a sense of balance. And I find having a purposeful activity gives me a boost all day.

I'm a very methodical person, so I like to weigh my pros and cons before making any decisions. When evaluating solutions, I think about what will work best for me and my circumstances. For instance, if someone's suggesting we take the train instead of driving, I'll consider factors such as travel time, cost, convenience, and how I feel about each option. If there's something that might be an advantage – say better scenery or more legroom – then I'll give it some thought, but ultimately, I'd need strong reasons why taking the train is preferred. It really comes down to weighing the advantages against the disadvantages, considering who else might be involved, and trusting my instincts about which one feels most sensible given all those elements.

I think incorporating feedback is crucial in making sure any solution we come up with is suitable for everyone involved. You know, people have different opinions and priorities, so taking the time to listen attentively and consider those views helps me tailor a plan that suits all parties. When someone shares concerns or suggestions, I make a note of them and ask clarifying questions until I'm satisfied we've got everything covered. Then, I weigh up what they said against our original aims and try to find common ground – sometimes there might be trade-offs, but generally, finding a middle way works best for most folks.

I think my approach to analytical thinking is quite traditional, really. I like to take a step back, gather all the facts and figures, and try to understand why something happens rather than just looking at the surface level. It's almost like breaking down a puzzle – once you have all the pieces out, you start seeing how everything fits together. For instance, when I was working as an admin assistant at the GP surgery, we'd be dealing with complex medical records and whatnot. I found myself getting bogged down in trying to figure out who said what, when, and where. But then I realized that instead of focusing solely on the individual components, it helped to look at the bigger picture – the flow of information, the relationships between different people and departments... You know? It's not always easy, mind you! Sometimes I still get frustrated when others rush past the obvious connections or overlook crucial details. But overall, I find taking the time to examine each piece thoroughly has made me more effective in solving problems and making informed decisions. And that's stuck with me even outside of work now too – whether it's tackling a tricky jigsaw puzzle or trying to unravel a mystery with the grandkids.

I find that taking a moment to sit quietly, put my pen to paper, and try to work out why something isn't quite clicking is really helpful. It's funny how sometimes putting words into writing helps me untangle thoughts and make sense of them all. Sometimes I'll even go back to books or articles we discussed previously, re-read what was said, and think about whether there's more to consider. My grandchild always says I'm very particular, but I suppose someone has to be!

I think identifying areas for growth is all about being honest with yourself and reflecting on your strengths and weaknesses. For me, it's been a bit hit-or-miss over the years, but I've found that taking my time to consider what I'm passionate about and where I feel like I need improvement helps a lot. In my case, I'd say one area I'd love to work on is staying more digitally savvy. As much as I appreciate the benefits of modern technology, I find it all so overwhelming at times! My grandkids always tease me about how ancient I am when it comes to using smartphones and computers. But seriously, there are some basics – like keeping track of digital payments, managing notifications, and navigating websites – that I struggle with. Another thing I'd like to improve upon is perhaps being more proactive about health-related issues. With my chronic conditions, it's easy to fall into bad habits or neglect checking my vitals regularly enough. So, setting reminders and making sure someone knows who I'll be talking to each day could really make a big difference. Lastly, while I have an excellent support network here locally, I sometimes wish we had access to certain resources or programs closer to our village. It might take a trip to the city, which isn't

I think my approach is quite simple, really. You know, I believe patience and curiosity play a big part in embracing new situations. When faced with something unfamiliar or challenging, I try to take a step back, breathe deeply, and ask myself why I'm feeling resistant or uncertain. Is there a particular aspect of it that's worrying me? Once I understand the reason, I start asking more questions and gathering information – sometimes that helps clarify things and makes them feel less daunting. It might sound obvious, but having some control over the situation itself also helps. Knowing what's expected of me, being able to plan ahead... these all contribute to making me feel more grounded and better equipped to handle whatever comes along. Now, I will admit, it's easier said than done! There'll be times when I still find myself getting nervous or frustrated. But generally speaking, I reckon that's because I value clarity and simplicity above everything else. If someone explains things clearly, shows me how it works, and gives me space to process and reflect, I tend to become much more receptive to new ideas and opportunities. And ultimately, I suppose it's also about trusting one's instincts. If I trust myself and have faith in my ability to navigate uncertainty, that makes all the difference.

I make sure to prioritise my leisure time, you know. As a morning person, I try to squeeze in some reading before lunchtime, usually while having a cuppa and enjoying some peace and quiet. It's lovely, really. And sometimes, if I'm feeling energetic, I might even take a walk afterwards to refresh myself before the afternoon slump sets in. Of course, it's all about finding that balance, isn't it? Nowadays, I must admit that our grandkids have been keeping me quite busy – always wanting to show off those little ones! But we still manage to carve out some dedicated time together, whether it's playing games, going on outings, or simply having a nice chat. Family is very important to me, so I wouldn't trade that for the world! As for how much time I actually spend reading...well, let's say I'd estimate about an hour or two most weekdays, maybe more if I've got a particularly engaging novel or biography. I like to think I'm making the most of every spare moment, though!

I usually find out about new authors or genres through recommendations from family members or close friends who share similar tastes. My granddaughter is always telling me about books she likes, which has introduced me to some wonderful writers like Lucy Mangan and Tracy Chevalier. Occasionally, I'll pick a book from my local library based on its cover or blurb, only to be pleasantly surprised by what turns out to be an excellent read. And sometimes, while browsing at a second-hand bookstore, I stumble upon something that catches my eye – that's how I discovered Agatha Christie!

I love putting my feet up with a cuppa and some music playing softly in the background! It's usually something calming like classical or nature sounds – perhaps an album by Elgar or some birdsong. Sometimes, I'll be knitting or sewing, making little scarves or hats for my grandkids. Other times, I might just sit quietly and let the rhythm wash over me, lost in thought. Music always seems to make everything feel more relaxing and peaceful.

I'm a big fan of getting lost in stories and information while commuting or during chores. Audiobooks have been lovely, really helped me fill those moments between cooking, gardening, or tidying up. They're so convenient! I like how I can still engage my brain even when my hands are busy elsewhere. As for podcasts, I find some of them quite informative and entertaining – history documentaries, true crime cases... That sort of thing. But I must admit, I do prefer having something tangible to refer to later, whether it's notes or actual books. Still, there's value in catching up on current events or being able to multitask. What's your take on these things, dear?

I love staying active! You know, people always say you should be too busy to exercise, but I think that's nonsense. When you're enjoying yourself, the time flies by. For me, my two main passions are gardening and reading. There's something so satisfying about getting out into nature, watching a seedling sprout, and nurturing it until it flourishes. It's meditative, really. As for reading, I'm absolutely hooked on historical fiction and biographies – there's nothing like losing myself in a good story or being transported by someone else's experiences. And I find it's an excellent way to relax and unwind before bed. Sometimes I'll even try to relate what I read to real-life issues we discuss at our local community centre or among friends. Makes everything feel more meaningful! Occasionally, I might join forces with some mates for a walk along the canal or visit the local park. We chat, laugh, and take in the fresh air. Those informal strolls always lift my spirits. Occasionally, I attend a bridge game night at the local village hall where everyone knows each other; those evenings bring back fond memories. It all adds up, doesn't it? A mix of structured routines and leisurely activities helps keep me feeling grounded and

I must say, I thoroughly enjoy spending time alone. It allows me to recharge my batteries, really think deeply, and focus on my hobbies like gardening and reading. There's something so peaceful about being surrounded by nature, watching a seedling sprout into a beautiful flower, or getting lost in a good book. Sometimes, I'll take myself out for a walk along our local canal path, enjoying the fresh air and the sound of birds singing. In those moments, I feel most alive, and I find solace in the simple pleasures in life. Of course, I'm grateful for regular interactions with loved ones too, but there's something special about having some quiet time all to myself.

I think the key is always to be open-minded and give them a go! You know, my late mother used to say that there's only one way to really find out what something tastes like, which was to try it yourself. So, I'll usually start with a simple dish, maybe some stir-fry or risotto, and work my way up to more exotic flavours later. And I love asking other people who have tried similar dishes for advice – sometimes they pick up on nuances we might miss ourselves. Of course, I'm not too keen on spicy food myself, so I tend to stick to milder options... But overall, I believe exploring different cuisines has broadened my horizons and introduced me to all sorts of interesting ingredients and techniques. As I said, it's all about being curious and having an adventure, isn't it?

I think creating variety is essential to keeping me happy and engaged. For one thing, my husband likes to plan our weekend outings a bit too much – he's always saying we should try something different! He's got us going on hikes, museum visits, and even took us on a surprise trip to Scotland last summer. It was lovely, although I must admit, I'm more suited to staying close to home. But seriously, I like mixing it up between some big plans and relaxing at home. Sometimes, I'll spend an afternoon tending to the garden; there's something therapeutic about getting my hands dirty and watching everything grow. And then I might have a busy morning spent writing letters to grandkids before heading off to volunteer at the local community center. When I read, I love switching between historical fiction novels and biographies – each offers such unique insights into human nature and history. Plus, it's nice having those contrasting worlds to escape into. Lastly, I've recently taken up baking again, which has been great fun. There's something satisfying about whipping up a cake or batch of cookies for the kids... er, I mean, the adults! They all seem to appreciate them almost as much as I do! Overall, finding balance between structured activities and downtime allows me

I have a little notebook where I jot down my plans and progress on various projects, including my hobbies like gardening and baking. It helps me keep track of what I need to buy or plant, so I'm prepared whenever I go out into the garden. My grandkids sometimes tease me because they know how much I love writing everything down – even though most of it ends up being scraps of paper stuck between cushions! But for me, having something tangible helps keep things organized and makes it easier to remember. Sometimes, I'll even make notes on my calendar on my phone, but handwritten is still my preferred method. There's something soothing about putting pen to paper, isn't there?

I'm a bit old-school, I suppose! *laughs* I like having a system, something that helps me keep track of my daily tasks, appointments, and whatnot. I have a large A4 notebook where I jot down reminders and notes, along with a calendar that hangs on the wall in my living room. It's quite simple really – I colour-code different areas of my life such as work, personal, and grandkids' birthdays. Whenever I need to make a list, I stick post-it notes all over the fridge until I tidy them away later! My husband always teases me about being so organised, saying I should be able to fit an entire university course into one of those tiny notebooks! But honestly, once everything is sorted out, I feel more relaxed knowing exactly where I am and what I need to do each day. Plus, it's nice to be able to refer back to past decisions or plans whenever needed. Of course, there's nothing too fancy here; I prefer keeping things straightforward rather than complicated!

I usually start my morning with a cuppa tea and have a quick glance at the news on my tablet while sitting comfortably on the sofa. After that, I like to tidy up any clutter and make sure everything is shipshape for the day ahead – which isn't much, mind! A bit of tidying, maybe some watering plants... gets me ready for whatever's planned. If there's nothing particular scheduled, I might take a short walk round the block or sit outside in the garden for a bit of fresh air. Then off I go, feeling relatively prepared for whatever comes my way.

I think keeping our house warm is a must during winter months, so we turn off all lights, radiators, and whatnot before going out. And, of course, using draught excluders and double glazing helps too. When I'm home in the evenings, my husband will sometimes go round turning everything off while he's watching telly – gets him into some sort of rhythm! We try to unplug stuff like laptops, kettles, and phones when not in use, although I know it's one of those things people say isn't really necessary anymore... Still, better safe than sorry, eh? In summer, opening curtains during the morning lets sunlight come in, which cools us down naturally, whereas closing them before bed keeps the heat in overnight. Our boiler's fairly efficient, and we make sure to have regular servicing done every couple of years. The kids used to tease me saying I was being "Mum" again, always worrying about saving money, but now I reckon it makes sense; we're getting older, and these little savings add up. Plus, our grandkids love playing outside more because there's been a lovely big tree planted outside the window where they can play safely. It's nice feeling we can give them a bit

To be honest, I'm still getting used to having a smart device in my house. My son got me an Amazon Echo thingy for Christmas, which is okay, I suppose. It's been helpful for playing music and setting reminders, but sometimes I find myself talking to it like it's a real person! It's all very convenient, but I prefer to stick to what I know – turning lights off with switches and using the kettle for tea. Still, I think it'll take some time to adjust, and I appreciate how it helps out with keeping track of schedules and appointments. Now, where did I put those batteries...?

I find prioritising competing goals is all about weighing what's most important in a situation. For me, staying relatively financially secure always comes first – I'm quite frugal and have worked hard to build some savings. But alongside keeping my grandkids happy, I'd say those two should be fairly close together. If one has to give way slightly, it might be having more disposable income available for them, perhaps by being mindful of expenses or finding cheaper alternatives where we can. Of course, there will still be sacrifices made along the way, but generally speaking, these three priorities sit nicely side-by-side for us.
